retrieved_docs.title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, drugs targeting IGF-II seemed to be very promising, as IGF-II is the most-up regulated gene in ACC (80% as described before). Preclinical studies antagonizing this pathway with pharmacological agents resulted in inhibition of growth in vitro and in vivo. This inhibition was more potent than that observed with the use of mitotane alone in decreasing xenograft growth, and the combination of IGF inhibition with mitotane resulted in greater antiproliferative effects than those observed with the use of single-agent treatment. These data establish the role of targeted disruption of IGF-1R signaling to obtain a therapeutic advantage when used with mitotane therapy or possibly other chemotherapeutics in ACC patients. [106] Recently, a phase 2 study used an IMCA12 (cixutumumab), a fully humanized IGF-IR antibody showed a lack of efficacy in a cohort of 19 patients. [107] Also, results of a huge phase 3 trial “GALACCTIC” with a highly specific IGF-IR inhibitor linstinib (OSI-906)in a cohort of 138 metastatic ACC have been recently published: the progression-free and overall survival did not differ between the linstinib and placebo groups. [108] However, in another study, 26 heavily pretreated ACC patients received cixutumumab with temsirolimus, an inhibitor of mammalian targets of IGF-1R signaling (mTOR inhibitor)weekly with restaging at eight weeks. Of these 26 patients, 11 (42%) had stable disease for greater than six months. [109] Finally, because of disease heterogeneity, it appears that using one single agent is not sufficient to induce an objective response. Trials with new targeted substances are underway.

retrieved_docs.title: Linaclotide -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Food effect: In a clinical trial, neither linaclotide nor its active metabolite MM-419447 was detected in plasma after oral intake of 290 mcg once-a-day dosage for 7 days in both fed and non-fed states in healthy volunteers.

retrieved_docs.title: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Atumelnant, an oral melanocortin type 2 receptor (ACTH receptor) antagonist, is being studied in a dose-finding open-label phase 2 study. [26] Initial findings suggested a profound and sustained decrease in the participants' morning androstenedione and 17-hydroxyprogesterone. Lu AG13909, an ACTH monoclonal antibody available in IV formulation, is being studied in a phase 1, open-label study to assess its effects on morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone, androstenedione, and ACTH levels ( NIHR. UCLH patient first in world to receive a new investigational drug in a phase 1 trial ). BBP-631, a gene-based therapy currently in early-stage development, aims to treat the condition most physiologically by promoting endogenous cortisol production by the adrenals  ( bridgebio. BridgeBio Pharma Reports Topline Results from Phase 1/2 Trial of Investigational Gene Therapy for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) ).

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Pradigastat: This is an orally administered diacylglycerol O -acyltransferase 1 (DGAT1) inhibitor. This drug reduces chylomicron-triglyceride secretion by catalyzing the final step in triglyceride synthesis. [26] [42] Evinacumab: This is a monoclonal antibody targeting ANGPTL3—an inhibitor of LPL and endothelial lipase. [26] [38] Volanesorsen: This is an antisense oligonucleotide targeting apoC-III, a complementary DNA strand to the apoC-III mRNA transcript. This binding leads to the degradation of apoC-III mRNA and a reduction in apoC-III protein levels. ApoC-III inhibits LPL, increases VLDL production, decreases VLDL degradation, reduces hepatic lipase activity, and impairs receptor-mediated triglyceride clearance by the liver. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Linaclotide -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Excretion: In fed and fasted healthy subjects, following oral administration of linaclotide 290 mcg daily for a week, a mean of 3% (fed) and 5% (fasted) of the dose was excreted as an active peptide in the feces, and an active metabolite MM-419447 was the predominant peptide recovered. According to the results of in vitro studies, MM-419447 binds to T84 cells with high affinity, which results in remarkable concentration-dependent intracellular accumulation of cyclic guanosine-3',5'-monophosphate (cGMP). In the results of animal studies, orally dosed MM-419447 increased intraluminal cGMP, significantly increased fluid secretion into the small bowel loops, and caused a dose-dependent acceleration of GI transit. These results demonstrate that the active metabolite contributes to linaclotide's pharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Experimental treatments encompass gene therapy utilizing lentiviral vectors, clustered regularly interspaced palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) gene editing, and investigational medications such as LGD-4665, anti-c-MPL minibodies, and diabodies. Researchers attempt to utilize lentiviral vector gene therapy to rectify c-Mpl variations; however, concerns persist regarding the potential leukemogenicity associated with this method. [10] Others are attempting to use CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing to correct the mutation. [47] Furthermore, LGD-4665, minibodies, and diabodies represent experimental pharmaceuticals that stimulate some variants of MPL receptors to enhance megakaryocyte proliferation. [30] [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Marburg Virus Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Antiviral Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Although no currently approved treatments exist, several pharmaceutical agents are in development. Galidesivir (BCX4430) is an antiviral, synthetic nucleoside analog that inhibits viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase. [37] RNA polymerase plays a crucial role in the viral replication process. Rodent models infected with MARV either through intraperitoneal injection or exposure to aerosolized virus received postexposure intramuscular administration of BCX4430, which conferred protection when initiated within 48 hours. Additionally, the efficacy of BCX4430 was explored in cynomolgus macaques. The macaques were infected with lethal doses of wild-type MARV and then administered BXC4430 intramuscularly twice daily for 14 days. While the control succumbed to the virus, all animals treated beginning 24 to 48 hours after infection survived. [38] A phase 1, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose-ranging study was completed in 32 subjects to evaluate the single-dose safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics of BXC4430 (NCT03800173). [37] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Evinacumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Renal Impairment: Although the definitive pathway of metabolism of evinacumab is unknown, it is not thought to undergo renal clearance. Through preliminary studies, patients with mild-to-moderate renal impairment demonstrated similar steady-state concentrations as those with normal renal function. Dose adjustment is unnecessary for mild-to-moderate renal impairment. Data is lacking for patients with severe renal impairment .

retrieved_docs.title: Patisiran -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Patisiran, a novel treatment for polyneuropathy associated with hATTR, is a small, double-stranded interfering RNA molecule (ALN-18328) formulated in a lipid covering using micro nanotechnology. [15] [16] This nanoparticle also contains 2 novel lipid excipients (PEG-C-DMG and DLin-MC3-DMA) to facilitate siRNA entry into the target cell cytoplasm. [17] Patisiran is administered via intravenous infusion and transported to the liver by blood. The apolipoprotein opsonized in the lipid capsule of the nanoparticle is recognized by the apolipoprotein-E receptors of hepatocytes, and the drug internalizes by endocytosis. The disruption of the nanoparticles in hepatocytes releases the pharmacologically active moiety siRNA. Then, siRNA processing by the ribonuclease, also known as the Dicer enzyme, occurs. This process involves cleaving the overhanging nucleotides on the edges of the siRNA. The processed siRNA binds to the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC).

retrieved_docs.title: Evinacumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibiting ANGPTL3, in turn, allows lipoprotein lipase and endothelial lipase to be uninhibited and aids in the breakdown of LDL. A phase 2 clinical trial demonstrated that the maximum dosage of evinacumab could decrease LDL levels by greater than 50%. [5] Evinacumab should be combined with other lipid-lowering medications, such as statins or PCSK9 inhibitors when treating HoFH. Additionally, patients should be advised to begin or continue lifestyle modifications such as diet and exercise to further aid in lowering LDL levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Phase 0 trials are optional, exploratory, first-in-human trials that are designed to determine whether the drug affects human subjects as expected from earlier pre-clinical investigations. These trials involve the administration of low, nontherapeutic doses of the study drug to a small number of participants (typically 10 to 15) and help gather preliminary data on the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of the drug in human subjects. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Mavacamten -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mavacamten is a novel, first-in-class oral medication approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as an allosteric modulator of cardiac myosin ATPase to treat obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) in patients. This medication is currently indicated for treating adults with symptomatic heart failure classified as New York Heart Association (NYHA) class II and III secondary to obstructive HCM and is aimed to improve functional capacity and alleviate symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Medullary Thyroid Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- RET-Specific Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with ret -mutant metastatic or nonresectable MTC, RET-specific inhibitors, including the following, are the preferred treatment options: Selpercatinib : Selpercatinib was evaluated in the phase 3 randomized LIBRETTO-531 trial, where it was compared to cabozantinib or vandetanib as first-line treatment for progressive ret -mutant MTC. At the 12-month follow-up, selpercatinib demonstrated a median PFS that was not yet reached, compared to 16.8 months for the comparator group. Pralsetinib: This RET-specific inhibitor was assessed in the phase 1 to 2 ARROW study, which included 92 patients with ret -mutant MTC. The overall response rate was 60% in patients who had received prior vandetanib and cabozantinib and 74% in patients with no previous treatment with these agents. [14] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib is an oral proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. [1] Ixazomib is the first orally dosed second-generation proteasome inhibitor to receive FDA approval and is used in combination with dexamethasone and lenalidomide in patients who have had prior treatment, allowing patients to be on a fully oral regimen, which has been proven to have a progression-free survival benefit. [2] [3] Ixazomib was approved based on the results of the phase III TOURMALINE-MM1 study, a worldwide double-blind and randomized study done on patients with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Linaclotide -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Linaclotide is metabolized in the GI tract. It is stable in the stomach's acidic (low pH) environment. By loss of terminal tyrosine moiety in the small intestine, linaclotide is metabolized to its principal and active metabolite MM-419447 (CCEYCCNPACTGC), which contributes to the pharmacological effects of the parent drug linaclotide. The systemic exposure to active peptides is minimal. In addition, low systemic and portal vein concentrations of linaclotide and metabolites confirm poor oral bioavailability. The reduction of disulfide bonds of both peptides takes place in the small intestine, where subsequently, they are proteolyzed and degraded into smaller peptides and naturally occurring amino acids. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Meclizine -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Achondroplasia is associated with activating mutations in the FGFR3 gene. Meclizine hydrochloride has demonstrated the ability to inhibit FGFR3 signaling and promote bone growth in an animal model of achondroplasia. Phase 1a clinical trial results have shown the safety and achievement of steady-state plasma concentrations of meclizine. In a subsequent phase 1b study involving children with achondroplasia, a 14-day repeated dose of meclizine was well-tolerated without serious adverse events. However, further research is required to confirm the safety and effectiveness of meclizine for managing achondroplasia. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1705
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serve as the parent class for the therapeutic agent nusinersen, indicating that nusinersen is a specific preparation within this broader category. Nusinersen, in turn, produces increased protein synthesis as a physiologic effect, reflecting its mechanism of action. Understanding that nusinersen belongs to the N [Preparations] family and that it induces increased protein synthesis helps clarify its role in clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a class of pharmaceutical preparations that includes the agents nedokromil and nusinersen. Both mupirocin and nusinersen produce the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, indicating that nusinersen, as a member of N [Preparations], shares this effect with mupirocin. Recognizing the categorical relationship of nedokromil and nusinersen within N [Preparations] and the shared protein‑synthesis‑enhancing action of nusinersen and mupirocin can aid in understanding their mechanisms and guiding therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a category of pharmaceutical preparations that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. As members of this preparation class, nedokromil and nusinersen share a common formulation lineage. Nusinersen, in particular, is noted for its physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, a mechanism that underlies its therapeutic action. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same N [Preparations] group while nusinersen uniquely promotes protein synthesis can inform clinicians about their relatedness and distinct pharmacologic profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: identified as an opioid analgesic, highlighting the therapeutic potential of kappa‑receptor targeting agents, which may offer analgesia with a different side‑effect profile compared with traditional mu‑agonists. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that opioid analgesics encompass a broad spectrum of dosage forms (injectable, oral solution, capsule), combination products, and both classic and investigational opioid molecules, all aimed at providing effective pain control across various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all agents that affect blood or body fluids. Crizanlizumab is further defined as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor and is placed within the therapeutic class of immunological agents. In clinical pharmacology it is regarded as an immunomodulator and also exhibits immunosuppressive activity, reflecting its capacity to alter immune pathways. Carglumic acid shares the broader classification of an agent affecting blood or body fluids, while dextrose monohydrate is likewise categorized in this group. Recognizing these interrelated classifications helps clinicians understand the mechanisms of action and therapeutic contexts of each agent, guiding appropriate patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 628
retrieved_docs.content: and mu‑antagonist activity, offering analgesia with a lower risk of respiratory depression. The peptide analogs enkefalin and the selective kappa‑agonist JT09 represent experimental or niche agents targeting specific opioid receptor subtypes, potentially providing analgesia with distinct side‑effect profiles. Understanding the diverse formulations, receptor actions, and clinical indications of these opioid analgesics is essential for optimizing pain management, minimizing adverse effects, and tailoring therapy to individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Anagrelide is a pharmacologic agent whose mechanism of action involves inhibition of phosphodiesterase enzymes; it functions as a phosphodiesterase inhibitor, a property that underlies its therapeutic effects in relevant clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: all classified as opioid analgesics. These substances share the primary therapeutic purpose of relieving moderate to severe pain, but they differ in potency, pharmacokinetic properties, routes of administration, and receptor selectivity. Injectable formulations such as meperidine, morphine, fentanyl, sufentanil, and methadone provide rapid systemic analgesia for acute or procedural pain, while oral solutions and combination capsules (e.g., acetaminophen‑butalbital‑caffeine‑codeine and acetaminophen‑caffeine‑dihydrocodeine) are designed for outpatient management of chronic or breakthrough pain and may offer additional antipyretic or sedative effects. Synthetic and experimental agents such as brorphine, etazene, and the peptide analogs enkefalin represent newer or investigational opioids that may have unique receptor binding profiles. JT09, specifically identified as a kappa‑opioid receptor agonist, exemplifies a subclass of opioids that can produce analgesia with a reduced risk of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist, indicating that it functions by blocking hormone receptors. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide hormone receptor antagonist mechanism of action and is also categorized as a hormone antagonist. These relationships show that both PD 134308 and atosiban share the functional property of antagonizing hormone receptors, a characteristic that is important for therapeutic approaches aimed at modulating hormone‑mediated physiological processes.
